item.title: Image attribution
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:

Original Source:Wikimedia Commons
Creator: By British Official Photographer [Public Domain]

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: As Ihp said, this is a public domain Wikipedia image, for the reasons Ihp stated. I've noticed that on this site, staff usually do everything, and its frowned on for the users to do anything themselves. But I noticed that you have not re-added the image yet. Should I do so now?

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Not the author, but a bit of digging found the image on Wikipedia here, under the Battle of the Somme section. The image page states that's it's in the public domain, as it's a photograph taken by the UK Government before 1957.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. One or more images on this article are non-compliant with that policy and have been removed. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM. Please do not readd the image until you have a source and list it in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like the idea of this one. My only gripe is how hard it is to read the first addendum. I'd like to see a footnote with a modern English interpretation of it. Otherwise it's an absolutely fascinating idea that's excellently well written

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 overall, but I think I would have liked that last entry better if you didn't explicitly say it's preparing for a gamma ray burst. Just saying it's trying to turn itself into a radiation shelter and leaving it at that would be better IMO as it would leave the event it was preparing for to the reader's imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That’s good to know; I’m not used to such an efficient system. In interactive fiction, anyone can add tags, and no one can change someone else’s tags, so sometimes really stupid tags stick around for years (like ‘bad game’ or something like that).

This system is much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the case with this wiki as well, the issue is that when an article has some tags but not all then it doesn't show up in the list of untagged pages that the tech team uses to know what articles they need to tag. I advise waiting for them to tag the article rather than trying to do it yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I just noticed it wasn’t showing up on any recently created lists.

In most wikis, tags can be added to a page without deleting old ones. If you let me know the important categories, I can go through and edit those in; this is my first time, and I’m still figuring things out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cleared the tags, if you're not going to add all relevant tags, it's best to leave such things to staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3762 is capable of moving at a rate of ██ meters using contraction waves

Xx meters per day? Year? Microsecond?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work for a first skip. The historical contexts are great flavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat as hell. Really impressed by how realistic that archaic writing feels.

item.title: Re: Not a draft
item.content:encoded: No, no it's fine. This is a great first SCP. My mistake

item.title: Re: Not a draft
item.content:encoded: This is my first time, so I keep making mistakes. Thanks for checking this article out though!

item.title: Re: Not a draft
item.content:encoded: Sorry then, must have gotten here as soon as soon as you posted it. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, it's up! Glad to have helped out.

item.title: Author's note
item.content:encoded:
This SCP was inspired by the prescient turtle in Michael Ende's 'Momo'.

Thanks to Leveritas for helpful criticism and to everyone who helped me with my previous attempt!

Most of the notes are from public domain texts, including Canterbury Tales, Shakespeare, the writings of Marcel Duchamp, and several Civil War diaries.

item.title: Not a draft
item.content:encoded:
This is not a draft; I was just copying it over according to the instructions found on the 'posting' tab of this page:

http://www.scp-wiki.net/how-to-write-an-scp

This version has already been critiqued in the drafts forum, and edits made accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please move your draft to the sandbox.
